name: Algebraic Operations with Alternates
description: Categorize expressions by operation type with multiple correct arrangements

models.prompt: Sort these algebraic expressions by their primary operation. Note: Some expressions may fit multiple categories.

name: Calculus: Derivatives vs Integrals
description: Four-category grid with calculus formulas and multiple placements

models.prompt: Categorize these calculus formulas by type. Some formulas can be used multiple times.

name: Geometric Shapes by Dimensions
description: Sort shapes with math formulas into 2D and 3D categories

models.prompt: Classify each shape as 2D (two-dimensional) or 3D (three-dimensional) based on their formulas.

name: Math Equations Classification
description: Categorize equations as true or false with LaTeX math rendering

models.prompt: Drag each equation to the correct category: True or False.

name: Bar Chart - Test Score Distribution
description: Interactive bar chart for analyzing student test scores with editable values

models.title: Class Test Score Distribution
models.prompt:
Data Analysis: The bar chart shows test scores for five students. Student C scored 15 points less than the class average of 85. Update the chart to show the correct score for Student C.

You can also add additional students if needed.
models.teacherInstructions: Scoring: Student C should have a score of 70 (85 - 15 = 70). Award full credit if students correctly calculate and enter 70 for Student C.
models.rationale: Student C's score should be 70 because the class average is 85 and Student C scored 15 points less: 85 - 15 = 70.

name: Histogram - Survey Response Frequencies
description: Histogram showing frequency distribution with all editable categories

models.title: Hours of Weekly Exercise Survey
models.prompt:
Statistics Problem: A survey asked 50 students how many hours they exercise per week. The results are shown in the histogram below.

However, some data was recorded incorrectly. Update the chart so that:

  • The "3-4 hours" category has 8 responses
  • The "5-6 hours" category has 12 responses
  • All frequencies sum to exactly 50 students
models.rationale:
To make the frequencies sum to 50:
Original sum: 6 + 10 + 9 + 5 + 10 + 8 = 48
We need: 6 + 10 + 9 + 8 + 12 + 5 = 50
Changed "3-4 hours" from 5 to 8 (+3), "5-6 hours" from 10 to 12 (+2), and "7+ hours" from 8 to 5 (-3) to balance the total.

name: Line Chart - Plant Growth with Math
description: Line chart with cross markers showing exponential plant growth over time

item.name: Line Chart - Plant Growth with Math

models.title: Plant Height Growth Over Time
models.prompt:
Biology Experiment: A plant grows according to the formula h(t) = 2t + 3, where h is height in centimeters and t is time in weeks.

The chart shows measurements for weeks 0-3. Calculate and plot the height for Week 4 and Week 5 using the growth formula.
models.teacherInstructions: Answer Key: Week 4: h(4) = 2(4) + 3 = 11 cm. Week 5: h(5) = 2(5) + 3 = 13 cm. Students should add these two data points to complete the growth pattern.
models.rationale:
Using the formula h(t) = 2t + 3:
Week 4: h(4) = 2(4) + 3 = 8 + 3 = 11 cm
Week 5: h(5) = 2(5) + 3 = 10 + 3 = 13 cm
The plant grows 2 cm each week with a starting height of 3 cm.

name: Basic Sentence Completion
description: Simple fill-in-the-blank sentence with text choices

models.prompt: Complete the sentence by dragging the correct words into the blanks.
models.markup: The {{0}} is the largest planet in our solar system, while {{1}} is the smallest.
models.rationale: Jupiter is the largest planet with a diameter of about 86,881 miles, while Mercury is the smallest with a diameter of only 3,032 miles.

name: Chemistry Balance with Duplicates
description: Balance chemical equations allowing duplicate use of coefficients

models.prompt: Balance the following chemical equations by dragging the correct coefficients. Some coefficients may be used more than once.
models.rationale: Chemical equations must be balanced so that the number of atoms of each element is the same on both sides. For water formation: 2H₂ + O₂ → 2H₂O. For ammonia synthesis: N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃.
models.teacherInstructions: This assesses understanding of stoichiometry and balancing chemical equations. Students must recognize that coefficients can be reused.

name: Mathematical Equations
description: Complete equations with mathematical expressions and symbols

models.prompt: Drag the correct mathematical expressions to complete each equation.
models.markup:
1. Quadratic formula: \(x = {{0}}\)

2. Pythagorean theorem: \(a^2 + b^2 = {{1}}\)

3. Area of a circle: \(A = {{2}}\)
models.rationale: These are fundamental formulas in mathematics. The quadratic formula solves equations of the form \(ax^2 + bx + c = 0\), the Pythagorean theorem relates the sides of a right triangle, and the circle area formula uses pi times the radius squared.

name: Word Problems with Arrays
description: Match word problems to visual array representations

models.prompt: Drag and drop the correct array into the box next to the matching word problem.
models.markup:
Word ProblemArray

Jamie is buying color pencils for an art project. There are 8 colored pencils in each pack. She buys 3 packs of colored pencils. How many colored pencils did she buy for her art project?

{{0}}

Mark has 36 jelly beans to split between 9 friends. How many jelly beans will each friend get?

{{1}}

Mr. Smith drinks 5 bottles of water each day. If there are 7 days in a week, how many bottles of water does Mr. Smith drink in 1 week?

{{2}}
models.rationale: Match each word problem to its corresponding visual array representation to solve multiplication and division problems.

name: Linear Function - Slope Intercept
description: Plot points and draw a line through them to represent y = 2x + 1

models.prompt:
Plot the line for the equation y=2x+1

Use points to mark where the line crosses each axis.
models.title: Graphing Linear Functions
models.rationale: The line y = 2x + 1 has a slope of 2 and y-intercept of 1. It crosses the y-axis at (0, 1) and passes through points like (2, 5).

name: Quadratic Function - Parabola
description: Graph a parabola by defining its vertex and a point on the curve

models.prompt:
Graph the parabola with vertex at (1, -4) that passes through the point (3, 0).

Hint: Click the vertex first (root), then click a point on the parabola (edge).
models.title: Parabola Graphing
models.rationale: The parabola has its vertex (minimum point) at (1, -4) and opens upward. Using the given point (3, 0), we can determine the exact shape of the parabola.

name: Sine Wave - Trigonometric Function
description: Create a sine wave with specific amplitude and frequency

models.prompt:
Graph a sine wave with amplitude 2 and period 2π.

The function is f(x)=2sin(x)
models.title: Sine Wave Graphing
models.rationale: A sine wave with amplitude 2 oscillates between -2 and 2. The period of 2π means one complete cycle occurs every 2π units along the x-axis. The root is at the origin (0,0) and the edge defines the amplitude at π/2 (approximately 1.57).
models.teacherInstructions: Students should understand that the root point marks where the sine wave crosses the x-axis, and the edge point defines both the amplitude and frequency of the wave.

name: Linear Inequality - Greater Than (Dashed Line)
description: Graph the solution set for y > 2x - 3

models.prompt: Graph the solution set for the inequality: <math>y > 2x - 3</math>
models.title: Linear Inequality with Dashed Boundary
models.rationale: The inequality y > 2x - 3 requires a dashed line (since it's > not ≥) at y = 2x - 3, with the region above the line shaded as the solution set.
models.teacherInstructions: Students should draw a dashed line through points like (-1, -5) and (2, 1), then select the region above the line as the solution set.

name: Linear Inequality - Less Than or Equal (Solid Line)
description: Graph the solution set for y ≤ -x + 4

item.name: Linear Inequality - Less Than or Equal (Solid Line)

models.prompt: Graph the solution set for the inequality: <math>y \leq -x + 4</math>
models.title: Linear Inequality with Solid Boundary
models.rationale: The inequality y ≤ -x + 4 requires a solid line (since it includes ≤) at y = -x + 4, with the region below and including the line shaded as the solution set.
models.teacherInstructions: Students should draw a solid line through points like (0, 4) and (4, 0), then select the region below the line as the solution set.

name: System of Two Inequalities
description: Graph the solution set for y < x + 2 AND y ≥ -2x + 1

models.prompt:
Graph the solution set for the system of inequalities:
y < x + 2
y \geq -2x + 1
models.title: System of Two Linear Inequalities
models.rationale: The system requires two boundary lines: y = x + 2 (dashed, since y < x + 2) and y = -2x + 1 (solid, since y ≥ -2x + 1). The solution set is the region that satisfies both conditions - below the dashed line and above/on the solid line.
models.teacherInstructions: Students should draw Line A as a dashed line for y = x + 2, then draw Line B as a solid line for y = -2x + 1. The solution set is the intersection region bounded by both inequalities.

name: Basic Multi-Select
description: Standard checkbox mode with multiple correct answers

choices.label: Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy

feedback.value: Correct! Photosynthesis is the process by which plants convert sunlight into glucose.

choices.rationale: Photosynthesis is performed by plants, algae, and some bacteria. It uses light energy to convert carbon dioxide and water into glucose and oxygen.

choices.label: Mitochondria are found only in plant cells

feedback.value: Incorrect. Mitochondria are found in both plant and animal cells.

choices.rationale: Mitochondria are the "powerhouses" of the cell and are present in nearly all eukaryotic cells, including both plants and animals.

choices.label: Cellular respiration breaks down glucose to produce ATP

feedback.value: Correct! Cellular respiration is how cells extract energy from glucose.

choices.rationale: Cellular respiration occurs in the mitochondria and converts glucose and oxygen into ATP (energy), carbon dioxide, and water. This is essentially the reverse of photosynthesis.

choices.label: Chloroplasts are the site of cellular respiration

feedback.value: Incorrect. Chloroplasts are where photosynthesis occurs, not cellular respiration.

choices.rationale: Chloroplasts contain chlorophyll and are responsible for photosynthesis in plant cells. Cellular respiration takes place in the mitochondria.

models.prompt: Biology Question: Select all true statements about cellular processes.

name: Math: Quadratic Equation
description: Algebra question with LaTeX notation testing quadratic formula knowledge

choices.label: \(x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2+4ac}}{2a}\)

feedback.value: Incorrect. The discriminant should be \(b^2 - 4ac\), not \(b^2 + 4ac\).

choices.rationale: The quadratic formula uses subtraction in the discriminant. The sign error would give incorrect solutions.

choices.label: \(x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}\)

feedback.value: Correct! This is the quadratic formula for solving equations of the form \(ax^2 + bx + c = 0\).

choices.rationale: The quadratic formula \(x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}\) is derived by completing the square on the general quadratic equation. The discriminant \(b^2-4ac\) determines the nature and number of solutions.

choices.label: \(x = \frac{b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}\)

feedback.value: Incorrect. The numerator should start with \(-b\), not \(b\).

choices.rationale: The sign of \(b\) is crucial. The formula must have \(-b\) in the numerator to correctly solve the equation.

choices.label: \(x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{a}\)

feedback.value: Incorrect. The denominator should be \(2a\), not just \(a\).

choices.rationale: The factor of 2 in the denominator comes from the process of completing the square. Without it, solutions would be doubled.

models.prompt: Algebra Question: Which of the following is the correct quadratic formula for solving \(ax^2 + bx + c = 0\)?

name: Math: Triangle Properties
description: Geometry question with LaTeX testing understanding of triangle theorems

choices.label: For a right triangle with legs \(a\) and \(b\) and hypotenuse \(c\): \(a^2 + b^2 = c^2\)

feedback.value: Correct! This is the Pythagorean theorem.

choices.rationale: The Pythagorean theorem states that in a right triangle, the square of the hypotenuse equals the sum of the squares of the other two sides. This is one of the most fundamental theorems in geometry.

choices.label: The sum of angles in any triangle is \(360^\circ\)

feedback.value: Incorrect. The sum of angles in a triangle is \(180^\circ\), not \(360^\circ\).

choices.rationale: The sum of interior angles in any triangle is always \(180^\circ\). The value \(360^\circ\) applies to quadrilaterals, not triangles.

choices.label: The area of a triangle is \(A = \frac{1}{2}bh\) where \(b\) is the base and \(h\) is the height

feedback.value: Correct! This is the standard formula for triangle area.

choices.rationale: The area of any triangle can be calculated using \(A = \frac{1}{2}bh\). This formula works because a triangle is half of a parallelogram with the same base and height.

choices.label: In an equilateral triangle with side length \(s\), the area is \(A = s^2\)

feedback.value: Incorrect. The area formula for an equilateral triangle is \(A = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}s^2\).

choices.rationale: The correct formula for an equilateral triangle is \(A = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}s^2\). The formula \(A = s^2\) would give the area of a square, not a triangle.

choices.label: For any triangle with sides \(a\), \(b\), and \(c\): the triangle inequality theorem states \(a + b > c\)

feedback.value: Correct! This is the triangle inequality theorem.

choices.rationale: The triangle inequality theorem states that the sum of any two sides of a triangle must be greater than the third side. This must be true for all three combinations of sides: \(a+b>c\), \(b+c>a\), and \(a+c>b\).

models.prompt: Geometry Question: Select all true statements about triangles and their properties.

partial.default: Close! Check whether the endpoints should be open or closed

incorrect.default: Incorrect. Remember: [a means closed at a, b) means open at b

name: Hope Is the Thing with Feathers
description: Basic passage configuration

item.name: Hope Is the Thing with Feathers

passages.title: Hope Is the Thing with Feathers
passages.subtitle: from The Complete Poems of Emily Dickinson
passages.author: by Emily Dickinson
passages.text:
00000Hope is the thing with feathers
00000That perches in the soul,
00000And sings the tune without the words,
00000And never stops at all,

50000And sweetest in the gale is heard;
00000And sore must be the storm
00000That could abash the little bird
00000That kept so many warm.

00000I’ve heard it in the chillest land,
10000And on the strangest sea;
00000Yet, never, in extremity,
00000It asked a crumb of me.

passages.title: Ineskeen Road, July Evening
passages.text:
The bicycles go by in twos and threes -
There's a dance in Billy Brennan's barn tonight,
And there's the half-talk code of mysteries
And the wink-and-elbow language of delight.
Half-past eight and there is not a spot
Upon a mile of road, no shadow thrown
That might turn out a man or woman, not
A footfall tapping secrecies of stone.

I have what every poet hates in spite
Of all the solemn talk of contemplation.
Oh, Alexander Selkirk knew the plight
Of being king and government and nation.
A road, a mile of kingdom. I am king
Of banks and stones and every blooming thing.

name: What sentences contain the character 6 in them?
description: Basic select text configuration

item.name: What sentences contain the character 6 in them?

models.prompt: What sentences contain the character 6 in them?
models.text:
If 'tweren't for sight and sound and smell,
I'd like the city pretty well,
But when it comes to getting rest,
I like the country lots the best.

Sometimes it seems to me I must
Just quit the city's din and dust,
And get out where the sky is blue,
And say, now, how does it seem to you?

tokens.text: If 'tweren't for sight and sound and smell,
I'd like the city pretty well,
But when it comes to getting rest,
I like the country lots the best.
Sometimes it seems to me I must
Just quit the city's din and dust,
And get out where the sky is blue,
And say, now, how does it seem to you?
